--- title: "Getting Started in R and RStudio" pagetitle: "Slides for Getting Started in R and RStudio" subtitle: "Parts 1 & 2 (4/30/26, 5/11/26)" institute: "OHSU OCTRI-BERD" author: "Jessica Minnier, Meike Niederhausen" # date: "04/30/2026" date-modified: "today" format: revealjs: incremental: false scrollable: true chalkboard: true theme: [sky_modified_smaller_font.scss] width: 1100 #default 1050 height: 825 #default 700 slide-number: true html-math-method: mathjax link-external-newwindow: true # html: # link-external-newwindow: true # toc: true execute: echo: true freeze: auto # re-render only when source changes # editor: visual editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ## Workshop webpage Please go to the webpage [https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/R_Intro_BERD_2026_04/](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/R_Intro_BERD_2026_04/){target="_blank"} to access workshop materials. ## Overview ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ### Part 1: 4/30/2026 - Intro to R & RStudio - Quarto documents & code chunks - R packages - Data: loading, viewing - Summarizing data - Quantitative - Categorical - Data visualizations - Quantitative: histograms, boxplots, scatterplots - Categorical: barplots - Missing data summary ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ### Part 2: 5/11/2026 - Data wrangling - Sorting data - Subsetting data (rows, columns) - Creating new variables in a dataset - Factor level variables - Replacing missing values with another value - Some more data viz (stratifying figures) - Resources ::: ::: # Introduction to R ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_welcome_R.png){fig-align="center"} ## What is R? ::: columns ::: {.column width="70%"} - A programming language - Focus on statistical modeling and data analysis - import data, manipulate data, run statistics, make plots - Useful for data science - Great visualizations - Also useful for most anything else you'd want to tell a computer to do - Interfaces with other languages i.e. python, C++, bash - Free and open source! ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](image/R_logo.png) ::: ::: For the history and details: [Wikipedia](https://bit.ly/1efFmaY){target="_blank"} - an interpreted language (run it through a command line) - procedural programming with functions - Why "R"?? Scheme inspired S (invented at Bell Labs in 1976) which inspired R since 1st letters of original authors (**free and open source!** in 2000) ## What is RStudio? ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} R is a programming language ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) = an interface to use R (with perks!) ::: ::: ![[Modern Dive](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#r-rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/r_vs_rstudio_1.png){fig-align="center"} ## Open RStudio on your computer (not R!) ![[Modern Dive](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#using-r-via-rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/01_md_r.png){fig-align="center"} ## RStudio anatomy ![[Emma Rand](http://www-users.york.ac.uk/~er13/17C%20-%202018/pracs/01IntroductionToModuleAndRStudio.html#what_are_r_and_rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/RStudio_Anatomy.svg){fig-align="center"} Read more about RStudio's layout in Section 3.4 of [Getting Used to R, RStudio, and R Markdown](https://ismayc.github.io/rbasics-book/3-rstudiobasics.html#rstudio-layout){target="_blank"} (Ismay and Kennedy 2021) # Creating reproducible reports with Quarto ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_rmarkdown_wizards_quarto.png){fig-align="center"} ## Quarto = `.qmd` file = Code + text $\to$ html `.qmd` files contain code + markdown syntax which can be "rendered" to other formats (html, pdf, Word, etc) ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} .qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} html output\ ![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: # Basic Quarto example ![[Artwork from "Hello, Quarto" keynote by Julia Lowndes and Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, presented at RStudio Conference 2022. Illustrated by Allison Horst.](https://allisonhorst.com/cetinkaya-rundel-lowndes-quarto-keynote){target="_blank"}](image/horst_quarto_moon_penguins.png){fig-align="center"} ## 1. Create a Quarto file (`.qmd`) **Two options:** 1. click on File $\rightarrow$ New File $\rightarrow$ Quarto Document...$\rightarrow$ OK, 2. or in upper left corner of RStudio click on ![](image/green_plus_create_file.png){width="70"} $\rightarrow$ ![](image/select_quarto_option.png){width="250"} ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} **Pop-up window selections:** - Enter a title and your name - Select `HTML` output format (default) - Engine: select `Knitr` - Editor: Select `Use visual markdown editor` - Click `Create` ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot_popup.png) ::: ::: ## 2. Create a Quarto file (`.qmd`) - After clicking on `Create`, you should then see the following in your editor window: ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"} ## 3. Save the Quarto file (`.qmd`) - **Save the file** by - selecting `File -> Save`, - or clicking on ![](image/01_Script_Save.png){width="33" height="24"} (towards the left above the scripting window), - or keyboard shortcut - PC: *Ctrl + s* - Mac: *Command + s* - You will need to specify - a **filename** to save the file as - ALWAYS use **.qmd** as the filename extension for Quarto files - the **folder** to save the file in ## 4. Create (render) html file We create the html file by **rendering** the .qmd file. Two options: 1. click on the Render icon ![](image/render_icon.png) at the top of the editor window, 2. or use keyboard shortcuts - Mac: *Command+Shift+K* - PC: *Ctrl+Shift+K* - A new window will open with the html output. - You will now see both .qmd and .html files in the folder where you saved the .qmd file. ::: callout-note - The template .qmd file that RStudio creates will render to an html file by default. - The output format can be changed to create a Word doc, pdf, slides, etc. ::: ## .qmd file vs. its html output ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} .qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} html output\ ![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: # 3 types of Quarto content 1. [*Code chunks*]{style="color:darkorange"}. <- [*What this workshop focuses on*]{style="color:purple"} 2. Text, lists, images, tables, links (covered in Quarto workshop) 3. YAML metadata (covered in Quarto workshop) *To learn more about working with Quarto, check out our BERD workshop* [*Creating Professional Presentations and Websites using R/Quarto*](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/Quarto_BERD_2025/){target="_blank"}. ![[Illustration by Alison Hill and Allison Horst, for RStudio.](https://allisonhorst.com/horst-hill-collaborations){target="_blank"}](image/horst_hedgehog_text_code.png){fig-align="center"} ## Code chunks ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} .qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} html output\ ![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: ## What does a code chunk look like? An empty code chunk looks like this: `Visual editor` ![](image/01_qmd_chunk_empty_visual.png) `Source editor` ![](image/01_qmd_chunk_empty_source.png) ::: callout-important Note that a code chunks start with ```` ```{r} ```` and ends with ```` ``` ````. Make sure there is no space before ```` ``` ````. ::: ## Enter and run code (1/2) - **Type R code** inside code chunks - **Select code** you want to run, by - placing the cursor in the line of code you want to run, - ***or*** highlighting the code you want to run ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - **Run selected code** by - clicking on the ![](image/01_Script_Run.png) button in the top right corner of the scripting window and choosing `Run Selected Line(s)`, - or typing one of the following key combinations: | | | |-----|----------------------| | Mac | **ctrl + return** | | PC | **command + return** | - *Where does the output appear?* ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](image/01_qmd_coding1a.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: ## Enter and run code (2/2) ::: {style="font-size: 80%;"} - **Run all code** in a chunk by - by clicking the play button in the top right corner of the chunk - The code output appears below the code chunk ::: ![](image/01_qmd_coding2.png){fig-align="center"} ::: callout-note - The output should also appear in the Console. - Settings can be changed so that the output appears only in the Console and not below the code chunk: - Select {{< fa solid gear >}} (to right of Render button) and then *Chunk Output in Console*. ::: ## Create a code chunk 3 options to create a code chunk 1. Click on ![](image/code_chunk_insert_icon.png) at top right of the editor window, or 2. [**Keyboard shortcut**]{style="color:darkorange"} | | | |-----|------------------------| | Mac | *Command + Option + I* | | PC | *Ctrl + Alt + I* | 3. `Visual editor`: Select `Insert` -\> `Executable Cell` -\> `R` ![](image/code_chunk_insert_visual.png) # R packages: installing & loading ![](image/tidyverse_celestial.jpg){fig-align="center"} ## What are R Packages?
A good analogy for R packages is that they\ are like apps you can download onto a smart phone
![[ModernDive Figure 1.4](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#packages){target="_blank"}](image/R_vs_R_packages.png){fig-align="center"} ## Installing packages - Packages contain additional functions and data Two options to install packages: 1. `install.packages()` *or* 2. The "Packages" tab in Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer window ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("janitor") # only do this ONCE, use quotes ``` ::: ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - **Only install packages once** *(unless you want to update them)* - Installed from [Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN)](https://cran.r-project.org/){target="_blank"} = package mothership ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](image/screenshot_packages_window.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: ## Packages used in this workshop ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="62%"} - **{tidyverse}** - A collection of many commonly used packages - **[*Core* {tidyverse}]{style="color:purple"}**: most commonly used {tidyverse} packages - These are automatically get loaded when {tidyverse} is loaded - Packages listed in [purple]{style="color:purple"} below are a part of the core - [Complete list of core {tidyverse} packages](https://tidyverse.tidyverse.org/#usage){target="_blank"}) - **[*Additional* {tidyverse}]{style="color:green"}** packages - The [additional packages](https://tidyverse.tidyverse.org/#packages){target="_blank"} are installed when the {tidyverse} package is installed, but they are not loaded when the {tidyverse} package is loaded. - They need to be loaded individually to use. - Packages listed in [green]{style="color:green"} below are additional {tidyverse} packages ::: ::: {.column width="8%"} ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} - For **loading** datasets: - [{readr}]{style="color:purple"} - [{readxl}]{style="color:green"} - For **summarizing** and **visualizing** data: - {janitor} - {gt} - {rstatix} - {gtsummary} - {naniar} - [{ggplot2}]{style="color:purple"} - for **wrangling** data - [{dplyr}, {tidyr}]{style="color:purple"} ::: ::: ## Install packages used in this workshop - Run the code below to install packages: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("tidyverse") # this takes a while to install install.packages("writexl") install.packages("rstatix") install.packages("janitor") install.packages("naniar") install.packages("gt") install.packages("gtsummary") ``` ::: - It is **VERY IMPORTANT** that you install these packages in order to run the code for the workshop! ## Load packages with `library()` function - Use the `library()` function to load each required package. - Packages need to be loaded *every* time you open Rstudio. - **The code to load packages MUST be in your qmd file.** - *Tip: at the top of your qmd file, create a chunk that loads all of the R packages you want to use in that file.* ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(janitor) # run this every time you open Rstudio ``` ::: ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - Check to see whether a package has been loaded: - Go to Packages tab - If box to left of package name is checked off, then it's loaded ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](image/screenshot_packages_window.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: ## Load packages used in this workshop - Run the code below in your R session ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) library(writexl) library(rstatix) library(janitor) library(naniar) library(gt) library(gtsummary) library(readxl) # part of tidyverse, but not the tidyverse core ``` ::: ::: callout-note - Note that the package names do not need quotes around them when using the `library()` function, but they are required when using the `install.packages()` function. ::: # Data basics in R ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_data_cowboy.png){fig-align="center"} ## Dataset example (toy data) ::: {style="font-size: 110%;"} - [**Use Excel to open the dataset `toy_data.xlsx` that is located in the data folder.**]{style="color:darkorange"} - Terminology - Rows are usually **observations** (i.e. a person's measurements) - Columns are usually **variables** or **measures** - How many observations are in this dataset? - What are the variable types in this dataset? ::: ## Import data into R ::: {style="font-size: 110%;"} - We can import data from many file types, including .csv, .txt., .xlsx, plus SAS or Stata files - Once imported, R typically stores data as [**data frames**]{style="color:darkorange"}, or [**tibbles**]{style="color:darkorange"} if using the `{tidyverse}` package. - These are examples of what we call [**object types**]{style="color:darkorange"} in R. - For our purposes, these are essentially the same, and we will often use the terms interchangeably. - These workshop slides will usually use the term **dataset** to generally refer to either a data frame or tibble. ::: ## Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: **point & click option** **SAVE THE CODE after using the point & click option** ::: columns ::: {.column width="40%"} - In RStudio's Files window, open the `data` folder and - then click on the dataset `toy_data.xlsx`. - Select the `Import Dataset...` option, - Import Options: Type in `NA` in the "NA:" box, and - then click on the `Import` button on the bottom right of the pop-up window. ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ![](image/load_data_point_click.png){fig-align="center"} ![](image/load_data_point_click_NA.png){fig-align="center"} ::: - **Copy the code from the console used to load the file into your script** (omitting the + sign): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA") ``` ::: ::: ## Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: **code option** - The point and click option used the `read_excel` function from the `readxl` package to load the xlsx file. - If comfortable with coding and the data, we can skip the point and click and directly write the code ourselves. - When loading the dataset, we need to specify where the xlsx file is located, which in this case is in the folder called `data`. - No matter which option we use, the [**code to load the data MUST be in your qmd**]{style="color:darkorange"}. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # library(readxl) # commented out since loaded above already toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA") ``` ::: - This code assigns the output of the `read_excel()` function (data) into an object named `toy_data` - The [`<-` is the assignment operator]{style="color:green"} - After loading, the name of the dataset object `toy_data` will be in your Environment tab - To learn more about loading in Excel files, read the [readxl intro vignette](https://readxl.tidyverse.org/){target="_blank"} - Also check out the readxl cheatsheet ([this will download a pdf](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/main/data-import.pdf){target="_blank"}). ## View the `toy_data` dataset ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - Run the `View` function below within RStudio. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} View(toy_data) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} - A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the `toy_data` dataset. - Note that the code chunk in the qmd file specifies `#| eval: FALSE` so that this code is not run while rendering the qmd file. ::: ::: ![](image/View_toy_data.png){fig-align="center"} ## What are the variable "types"? | R variable type | Description | |------------------|------------------------------------------------------| | `dbl`: double | numbers; also `num` or `int` for numbers or integers | | `chr`: character | text, "strings" | | `fct`: factor | categorical variables stored with levels (groups) | | `lgl`: logical | boolean (TRUE, FALSE) | ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} glimpse(toy_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 20 Columns: 11 $ id 335340, 638618, 922382, 923122, 923963, 925603,… $ age "17 years old", "16 years old", "14 years old",… $ sex "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Ma… $ grade "10th", "9th", "9th", "9th", "10th", "10th", "1… $ race4 "White", NA, "White", "White", "Black or Africa… $ bmi 27.5671, 29.3495, 18.1827, 21.3754, 19.5988, 22… $ weight_kg 66.23, 84.82, 57.61, 60.33, 63.50, 70.31, 45.36… $ text_while_driving_30d NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, "0 days… $ smoked_ever NA, "Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No… $ bullied_past_12mo NA, NA, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, … $ height_m 1.550000, 1.699999, 1.779999, 1.680001, 1.79999… ``` ::: ::: ## Getting to know the dataset ### How many rows and columns? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # dim is for dimensions # Output: # of rows, # of columns dim(toy_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20 11 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} nrow(toy_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ncol(toy_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 11 ``` ::: ::: ### What are the variable names? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} names(toy_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "id" "age" "sex" [4] "grade" "race4" "bmi" [7] "weight_kg" "text_while_driving_30d" "smoked_ever" [10] "bullied_past_12mo" "height_m" ``` ::: ::: # The Youth Risk Behavior Surveillance System (YRBSS) Data from the CDC's [Youth Risk Behavior Surveillance System (YRBSS)](https://www.cdc.gov/healthyyouth/data/yrbs/index.htm){target="_blank"} - complex survey data - national school-based survey - conducted by CDC and state, territorial, and local education and health agencies and tribal governments - monitors six categories of health-related behaviors - that contribute to the leading causes of death and disability among youth and adults - including alcohol & drug use, unhealthy & dangerous behaviors, sexuality, and physical activity - see [Questionnaires](https://www.cdc.gov/healthyyouth/data/yrbs/questionnaires.htm){target="_blank"} - the data in `yrbss_demo.xlsx` are a subset of data in the R package [`yrbss`](https://github.com/hadley/yrbss){target="_blank"}, which includes YRBSS from 1991-2013 # Practice time! (part 1) Open the Excel file `yrbss_demo.xlsx` in the *data* *folder* to familiarize yourself with it. Use R code to answer the following questions. 1. Load the dataset `yrbss_demo.xlsx`. Make sure the name of the loaded data is `yrbss_data`. 2. What are the column names? 3. How many rows and columns are in `yrbss_data`? 4. Which variables are numeric (quantitative)? 5. Which variables are character? # Load the YRBSS data - Open the Excel file `yrbss_demo.xlsx` in the *data* *folder* to familiarize yourself with it. - This is a larger version of the toy data above, but it is still a subset of the full data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # library(readxl) # note we are saving the data from the Excel file in our environment # with the object name yrbss_data yrbss_data <- read_excel("data/yrbss_demo.xlsx") ``` ::: First look at the data: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} glimpse(yrbss_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 20,000 Columns: 8 $ record 931897, 333862, 36253, 1095530, 1303997, 261619, 926649, 1309… $ age "15 years old", "17 years old", "18 years old or older", "15 … $ sex "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Male", "… $ grade "10th", "12th", "11th", "10th", "9th", "9th", "11th", "12th",… $ race4 "White", "White", "Hispanic/Latino", "Black or African Americ… $ race7 "White", "White", "Hispanic/Latino", "Black or African Americ… $ bmi 17.1790, 20.2487, NA, 27.9935, 24.4922, NA, 20.5435, 19.2555,… $ stweight 54.43, 57.15, NA, 85.73, 66.68, NA, 70.31, 58.97, 123.38, NA,… ``` ::: ::: - After loading the dataset, you will see `yrbss_data` in the Data list of the Environment window. ## View the `YRBSS` dataset - Run the `View` function below within RStudio. - A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the `yrbss_data` dataset. - Note that the code chunk specifies `#| eval: FALSE` so that this code is not run while rendering the .qmd file. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} View(yrbss_data) ``` ::: First 20 rows shown below: ![](image/View_yrbss_data.png){fig-align="center"} # Summarizing data and `|>` - Often we want to summarize columns of data - Quantitative data and categorical/text data will be summarized differently - We also introduce the [pipe symbol]{style="color:darkorange"} `|>`, which is used to string together functions The [pipe is read "and then"]{style="color:darkorange"} and tells R the next function will be applied to the data on the left side of the pipe: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> head() # shows top rows ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 8 record age sex grade race4 race7 bmi stweight 1 931897 15 years old Female 10th White White 17.2 54.4 2 333862 17 years old Female 12th White White 20.2 57.2 3 36253 18 years old or older Male 11th Hispanic/Lati… Hisp… NA NA 4 1095530 15 years old Male 10th Black or Afri… Blac… 28.0 85.7 5 1303997 14 years old Male 9th All other rac… Mult… 24.5 66.7 6 261619 17 years old Male 9th All other rac… NA NA ``` ::: ::: # Summarizing quantitative data ## `get_summary_stats()` from the `{rstatix}` package - The **`{rstatix}` package** has a useful function called `get_summary_stats()` for quick statistics of quantitative data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # library(rstatix) yrbss_data |> get_summary_stats( bmi, stweight, type = "common" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 10 variable n min max median iqr mean sd se ci 1 bmi 13542 13.2 53.9 22.3 5.52 23.5 4.99 0.043 0.084 2 stweight 13443 27.7 181. 64.0 19.0 67.5 16.9 0.145 0.285 ``` ::: ::: - The `type` options are - `type = c("full", "common", "robust", "five_number", "mean_sd", "mean_se", "mean_ci", "median_iqr", "median_mad", "quantile", "mean", "median", "min", "max")` ## Stratified summaries (and more on `|>`) - Now let's **summarize the BMI and weight variables by a strata subgroup**. - We do this with the `group_by()` function - Notice that we now have two `|>`: - `data |> group_by(...) |> get_summary_stats(...)` - With the pipe symbol `|>` we can **string together many functions** in the order we want them applied. Example: - first tell R what dataset to use, - then have R group (stratify) the data by `sex`, - and then calculate summary statistics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> group_by(sex) |> get_summary_stats( bmi, stweight, type = "common" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 11 sex variable n min max median iqr mean sd se ci 1 Female bmi 6622 13.2 53.3 22.1 5.33 23.3 4.96 0.061 0.119 2 Female stweight 6542 27.7 181. 59.0 15.9 61.7 14.2 0.175 0.343 3 Male bmi 6920 13.2 53.9 22.5 5.51 23.7 5.01 0.06 0.118 4 Male stweight 6901 35.4 181. 69.4 20.0 73.1 17.4 0.209 0.41 ``` ::: ::: ## Stratify by 2 variables - ``` ::: ::: # Two pipes in R: `|>` vs `%>%` ![](image/captchas_pipe_wickham.png){fig-align="center" width="40%"} [Image from [https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hadleywickham_rstats-activity-7435450431968919552-hY19](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hadleywickham_rstats-activity-7435450431968919552-hY19){target="_blank"}]{.smaller} ## Two pipes in R: `|>` vs `%>%` - Both pipes are used to write code from **left to right** - by passing the result of one step into the next. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Background - The newer **base R pipe `|>`** was introduced in 2021 with the release of R 4.1.0. - The original **magrittr pipe `%>%`** - comes from the {magrittr} package and - became popular through the {tidyverse}.
- Using the **base R pipe `|>`** does *not* require loading the {tidyverse} package (which includes {magrittr}), - while using the **magrittr pipe `%>%`** does require {tidyverse}. ### Similarities - They work the same when the left-hand side becomes the **first argument** of the next function. - Example: ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} Using `|>` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> dim() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20000 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Using `%>%` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data %>% dim() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20000 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ### Differences *When do `|>` and `%>%` behave differently?* ::: {.panel-tabset} #### [Simple example]{style="color:purple"} ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - `|>` is **stricter** and expects an actual function call on the right ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> dim() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20000 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> dim ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in dim: The pipe operator requires a function call as RHS (:1:15) ``` ::: ::: - `yrbss_data |> dim()` works, but `yrbss_data |> dim` does not: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} With `%>%`, we can omit the `()` after the function name: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data %>% dim() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20000 8 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data %>% dim ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 20000 8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: #### [Advanced example]{style="color:purple"} - Code below runs a linear regression model with dependent variable `bmi` and independent variable `age`. - Using `%>%`: - note that for the data argument a `.` is used as the placeholder for the dataset `yrbss_data` since it's already being "fed into" the model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data %>% lm(bmi ~ age, data = .) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = bmi ~ age, data = .) Coefficients: (Intercept) age13 years old age14 years old 22.3834 -1.3292 -0.1210 age15 years old age16 years old age17 years old 0.5009 1.0989 1.5347 age18 years old or older 2.1176 ``` ::: ::: - Using `|>`: - Using a `.` as as a placeholder for the data does not work with the newer base R `|>`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> lm(bmi ~ age, data = .) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(mf, parent.frame()): object '.' not found ``` ::: ::: - Starting with R 4.3.0, we can use a placeholder but it's an underscore`_`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> lm(bmi ~ age, data = _) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = bmi ~ age, data = yrbss_data) Coefficients: (Intercept) age13 years old age14 years old 22.3834 -1.3292 -0.1210 age15 years old age16 years old age17 years old 0.5009 1.0989 1.5347 age18 years old or older 2.1176 ``` ::: ::: #### [More advanced examples]{style="color:purple"} See `Learn more` tab above for resources with more examples. ::: ### For this workshop We will use `|>` because - it is built into R, - requires no extra package, and - works well for most {tidyverse} code you will write as beginners Our older workshops and many reference materials use `%>%`. ### Learn more - [Differences between the base R and magrittr pipes](https://tidyverse.org/blog/2023/04/base-vs-magrittr-pipe/){target="_blank"} - Tidyverse blog post by Hadley Wickham - [Discussion of differences on Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/67633022/what-are-the-differences-between-rs-native-pipe-and-the-magrittr-pipe){target="_blank"} - [R 4.1.0 pipe overview](https://www.jumpingrivers.com/blog/new-features-r410-pipe-anonymous-functions/){target="_blank"} - [Comparing pipes: Base-R |> vs {magrittr} %>%](https://albert-rapp.de/posts/31_pipes_compared/31_pipes_compared){target="_blank"} - this resource includes examples and - also how to change the "default" pipe in RStudio from `%>%` to `|>` (keyboard shortcut will use `|>`) ::: # Summarizing categorical data: frequency tables - We will be creating frequency tables using the **`tabyl()` function from the `{janitor}` package** (loaded above). ## Frequency `tabyl()` for one variable Default table: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> tabyl(age) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` age n percent valid_percent 12 years old or younger 137 0.00685 0.00692094 13 years old 96 0.00480 0.00484971 14 years old 2026 0.10130 0.10234908 15 years old 4290 0.21450 0.21672139 16 years old 4924 0.24620 0.24874968 17 years old 4988 0.24940 0.25198282 18 years old or older 3334 0.16670 0.16842637 205 0.01025 NA ``` ::: ::: Make the output prettier using `adorn_` options and adding on `gt()`: ::: columns ::: {.column width="55%"} With adornments: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> tabyl(age) |> adorn_totals("row") |> adorn_pct_formatting(digits=2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` age n percent valid_percent 12 years old or younger 137 0.69% 0.69% 13 years old 96 0.48% 0.48% 14 years old 2026 10.13% 10.23% 15 years old 4290 21.45% 21.67% 16 years old 4924 24.62% 24.87% 17 years old 4988 24.94% 25.20% 18 years old or older 3334 16.67% 16.84% 205 1.03% - Calculate the minimum, median, and maximum BMI and weights of the participants in the YRBSS data, now stratified by age. # Data visualization - This is a *very brief* introduction to creating plots using the `{ggplot2}` package. - The `{ggplot2}` package gets loaded with the `{tidyverse}` and thus we do not need to load it separately. ::: columns ::: {.column width="40%"} ![[ggplot](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/index.html){target="_blank"}](image/ggplotlogo.png){fig-align="center" height="400"} ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/){target="_blank"}](image/ggplot2_masterpiece.png){fig-align="center" height="400"} ::: ::: # Visualizing quantitative variables - What is the distribution shape of a quantitative variable? - Does the distribution shape of a quantitative variable change when stratified by a categorical variable? - Is there an association between two quantitative variables? ## Histograms ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # specify the dataset # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used aes(x = bmi)) + geom_histogram() # type of plot ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-35-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Boxplots - Recall that the whiskers' ends are the minimum and maximum values - The box is from the first to third quartiles, with the line inside the box at the median ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} **BMI**:\ horizontal boxplot (specify `x = ...`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # dataset # aes: specify vars being used aes(x = bmi)) + geom_boxplot() # type of plot ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-36-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Weight**: vertical boxplot (specify `y = ...`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # use y instead of x in aes for vertical: aes(y = stweight)) + geom_boxplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Stratified boxplots (plus axis labels & title!) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes( # horizontal boxplots for BMI (x=): x = bmi, # stratified vertically (y=) by age: y = age )) + geom_boxplot() + # Add on labels and a title: labs(x = "BMI", y = "Age", title = "BMI distributions by age category" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-38-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Add color using `fill` - Also remove the `NA` category with `drop_na()` (we will talk about this in later slides) - What's the difference in use of `fill=` here? ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot( data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW! aes( x = bmi, y = age, fill = age # NEW! )) + geom_boxplot() + # remove legend: theme(legend.position="none") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-39-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot( data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW! aes( x = bmi, y = age, fill = sex # NEW! )) + geom_boxplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-40-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Scatterplots ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # specify the dataset # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used aes(x = stweight, y = bmi )) + geom_point() # type of plot ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-41-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Scatterplot with color-coded dots ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used aes(x = stweight, y = bmi, color = sex, )) + geom_point(alpha = .2) # alpha controls transparency of the points ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-42-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Color vs. fill vs. alpha ::: {style="font-size: 110%;"} - `color` (or `colour`) is used for lines and outlines - `fill` is used for interiors - Note: geom_point() shape default is a point with only an outline and no interior -\> *we used color and not fill* - `alpha` specifies the opacity / transparency - Values range from 0 to 1 - lower alphas are more transparent - See ggplot webpage [Colour related aesthetics: colour, fill, and alpha](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/aes_colour_fill_alpha.html){target="_blank"} for more information ::: # Visualizing categorical variables ## Barplots By default, barplots display counts (frequencies) on the vertical axis: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(x = age)) + geom_bar() + # specify plot type # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -45, hjust = 0)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-43-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Barplots with percents To show percents, the code is more complicated. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(x = age)) + # specify aesthetics within the barplot to show proportions geom_bar(aes(y = after_stat(prop), group = 1)) + # Next line converts y-axis labels to percentages instead of proportions scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) + # "dodge" the x-axis labels scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2)) + labs(y = "Percent") # default label is prop ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-44-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Barplots with 2 variables: segmented bar plots ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} Stacked bars showing counts: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(y = age, # specify stacking variable: fill = grade)) + geom_bar() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-45-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} For proportions within each bar:\ (added `position = "fill"` within `geom_bar()`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(y = age, # specify stacking variable: fill = grade)) + geom_bar(position = "fill") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-46-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: - Note that by using `y = age` instead of `x = age`, - the bars of the figure are now horizontal instead of vertical. - With the horizontal bars, we can see the long labels for each category. ## Side-by-side barplots (dodged) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(x = age, # specify variable for colors fill = grade)) + geom_bar(position = "dodge") + # make dodged bars # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-47-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Saving a ggplot as an image file ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} First, create a plot and save it as an R object: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} myplot <- ggplot(data = yrbss_data, aes(x = age, # specify variable for colors fill = grade)) + geom_bar() + # angle the x-axis labels #so that they are not overlapping theme( axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0)) myplot ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-48-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} Save the plot as a pdf (or "jpeg", "tiff", "png", etc.) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggsave( filename = "output/myplot.pdf", plot = myplot, device = "pdf", width = 5, height = 3, units = "in", dpi = 200) ``` ::: Can specify the dpi when saving, many journals have dpi requirements. See [`ggsave` webpage]{https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ggsave.html} for more details. ::: ::: ## \* More ggplot topics - Change the [**theme**](https://ggplot2-book.org/themes){target="_blank"} of the plot - This includes the color scheme, text font and size, and other options. - There are many existing themes to choose from. - Arrange multiple plots as one - [patchwork](https://patchwork.data-imaginist.com/){target="_blank"} package - Claus O. Note that order of variables in `arrange()` matters! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> arrange(record, age) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 8 record age sex grade race4 race7 bmi stweight 1 30592 NA NA 2 30593 9th Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… NA NA 3 30595 16 years old 10th White White NA NA 4 30599 12 years old or younger Male Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… NA NA 5 30601 12 years old or younger Male 11th White White NA NA 6 30604 12 years old or younger Male 9th Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… NA NA 7 30605 12 years old or younger Male 9th All other ra… Asian NA NA 8 30607 12 years old or younger Male 10th All other ra… Am I… NA NA 9 30608 12 years old or younger Male 9th All other ra… NA NA 10 30612 14 years old Male 9th All other ra… NA NA # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## `arrange()` after `count()`-ing categorical data - ℹ 14,677 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Filtering requires a little logic - We can chain multiple criteria using the [`&` (AND)]{style="color:darkorange"} or [`|` (OR) operators]{style="color:darkorange"}. - But we need to review a little logic before we do this. - If we wanted to return participants who identified as - `Female` **and** are in 10th grade, - we would use an `&` to chain these criteria together: ``` sex == "Female" & grade == "10th" ``` - Note that *SPELLING AND CASE MATTER*! - You must write the name of the column exactly and - also the value (i.e. "Female" not "female") exactly. ## Filtering with `&` (AND) criteria ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> filter(sex == "Female" & grade == "10th") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2,332 × 8 record age sex grade race4 race7 bmi stweight 1 1317010 18 years old or older Female 10th Black or Afr… Blac… 28.3 61.2 2 1315996 17 years old Female 10th White White 16.3 44.4 3 1315971 17 years old Female 10th Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… 19.6 56.7 4 1315934 17 years old Female 10th Black or Afr… Blac… 23.5 70.3 5 1315928 17 years old Female 10th Black or Afr… Blac… 21.0 45.4 6 1315926 17 years old Female 10th Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… 25.9 66.2 7 1315884 17 years old Female 10th White White 51.4 132. 8 1315816 17 years old Female 10th All other ra… Mult… 22.2 59.0 9 1315792 17 years old Female 10th Hispanic/Lat… Hisp… 21.7 61.2 10 1315776 17 years old Female 10th Black or Afr… Blac… 20.6 50.8 # Suppose we want to remove the `10th` grade category. - However this column has missingness in it as we can see with `tabyl()`. - When we try to filter using `!="10th"` we lose our `NAs`! - That isn't really expected. - You need to account for this with an *or* `|` statement: ::: columns ::: {.column width="45%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> tabyl(grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade n percent valid_percent 10th 4907 0.24535 0.2504338 11th 4891 0.24455 0.2496172 12th 4577 0.22885 0.2335919 9th 5219 0.26095 0.2663570 406 0.02030 NA ``` ::: ::: Lost the `NA`'s in the filtering below! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> filter(grade != "10th") |> tabyl(grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade n percent 11th 4891 0.3330156 12th 4577 0.3116361 9th 5219 0.3553483 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="55%"} Updated code to keep the `NA`'s when filtering: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> # add an | (OR) statement # to keep in the NA values filter((grade != "10th") | is.na(grade)) |> tabyl(grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade n percent valid_percent 11th 4891 0.32405751 0.3330156 12th 4577 0.30325316 0.3116361 9th 5219 0.34578944 0.3553483 406 0.02689989 NA ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: # Subset columns: `select()`ing data ![[data transformation cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/master/data-transformation.pdf){target="_blank"}](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_subset_cols.png){fig-align="center"} ## Selecting columns using `select()` - The `select()` function allows us to select variables or columns from our dataset: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### basic example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(record, age, race4) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 3 record age race4 1 1317069 18 years old or older Black or African American 2 1317067 18 years old or older White 3 1317066 18 years old or older White 4 1317065 18 years old or older White 5 1317064 18 years old or older White 6 1317063 18 years old or older White 7 1317062 18 years old or older White 8 1317058 18 years old or older Black or African American 9 1317057 18 years old or older All other races 10 1317056 18 years old or older White # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: ### remove columns - If we want to select everything *except* specified variable(s), - we can use a `-` in front of the variable(s) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(-race4, -race7) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 6 record age sex grade bmi stweight 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th 24.4 69.0 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th 18.8 49.9 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th 32.1 90.7 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th 21.2 56.2 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th 23.5 68.0 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th 21.7 52.2 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th 24.8 61.2 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th 17.7 49.9 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th 19.8 44.4 10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th 21.7 61.2 # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: ### consecutive columns - Select consecutive columns with `var_first:var_last` - Make sure you know the order of your columns so that this is correct ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(age:grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 3 age sex grade 1 18 years old or older Female 12th 2 18 years old or older Female 12th 3 18 years old or older Female 12th 4 18 years old or older Female 12th 5 18 years old or older Female 12th 6 18 years old or older Female 12th 7 18 years old or older Female 12th 8 18 years old or older Female 11th 9 18 years old or older Female 12th 10 18 years old or older Female 12th # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: ### rearrange columns - `select()` and `everything()` are a useful combination for some quick rearranging of columns: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(record, bmi, everything()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 8 record bmi age sex grade race4 race7 stweight 1 1317069 24.4 18 years old or older Female 12th Black or Afr… Blac… 69.0 2 1317067 18.8 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 49.9 3 1317066 32.1 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 90.7 4 1317065 21.2 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 56.2 5 1317064 23.5 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 68.0 6 1317063 21.7 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 52.2 7 1317062 24.8 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 61.2 8 1317058 17.7 18 years old or older Female 11th Black or Afr… Blac… 49.9 9 1317057 19.8 18 years old or older Female 12th All other ra… Asian 44.4 10 1317056 21.7 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 61.2 # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: - There's also a function [`relocate()` to move around your column order](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/relocate.html){target="_blank"}. - The three options below all create the same results: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> relocate(record, bmi, everything()) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "bmi" "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" "race7" [8] "stweight" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .after = record) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "bmi" "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" "race7" [8] "stweight" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .before = age) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "bmi" "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" "race7" [8] "stweight" ``` ::: ::: ::: ## `{tidyselect}` - There are ways to select columns by "searching" for them. - These are called `{tidyselect}` helpers. - See more examples from the [{tidyselect} webpage](https://tidyselect.r-lib.org/reference/language.html){target="_blank"}. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### `contains` - For instance, you can see how this might be useful, where we select all columns where the column name includes the word "race": ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(contains("race")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 2 race4 race7 1 Black or African American Black or African American 2 White White 3 White White 4 White White 5 White White 6 White White 7 White White 8 Black or African American Black or African American 9 All other races Asian 10 White White # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: ### `starts_with`, `ends_with` - Select all columns starting with "race": ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(starts_with("race")) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "race4" "race7" ``` ::: ::: - Select all columns ending with "e": ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(ends_with("e")) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "age" "grade" ``` ::: ::: ### Select columns by data type - We can also select variables based on data types - For instance, `select(where(is.numeric))` selects all numeric columns - (`where()` is a helper function that returns columns where the inside function is `TRUE`, - in this case `is.numeric` returns the `TRUE`/`FALSE`) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(where(is.numeric)) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "bmi" "stweight" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> select(where(is.character)) |> names() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" "race7" ``` ::: ::: ::: ## The difference between `filter()` and `select()` - One thing to keep in mind is that: - `filter()` works on [rows]{style="color:darkorange"} (think FILTER in Excel!), and - `select()` works on [columns]{style="color:darkorange"} (select your relevant variables) Keep that in mind! ## Renaming with `rename()` - You can rename columns with `select()` but it's a bit easier to do this with the `rename()` function. - You just need to remember **`new_name = old_name`** ordering. - One equal sign will suffice! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> rename(id = record, race_cat = race4, weight = stweight) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20,000 × 8 id age sex grade race_cat race7 bmi weight 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th Black or Afric… Blac… 24.4 69.0 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 18.8 49.9 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 32.1 90.7 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 21.2 56.2 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 23.5 68.0 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 21.7 52.2 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 24.8 61.2 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th Black or Afric… Blac… 17.7 49.9 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th All other races Asian 19.8 44.4 10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th White White 21.7 61.2 # ℹ 19,990 more rows ``` ::: ::: *Note that [we did not save the changes above]{style="color:darkorange"}, and thus we haven't actually renamed the variables in the dataset `yrbss_data`!* ## Saving our results as a file - Let's save some processed data in the `output/` directory. - We'll save it as an `xlsx` file so that we can open it in Excel. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # chain together several data transformation steps with |> # use <- to assign the new data to a different name to avoid overwriting the original dataset processed_data <- yrbss_data |> select(-race7) |> rename(id = record, race_cat = race4, weight = stweight) |> filter(sex == "Female") glimpse(processed_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 9,592 Columns: 7 $ id 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 1317062… $ age "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years o… $ sex "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "… $ grade "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11th… $ race_cat "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whit… $ bmi 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.8448… $ weight 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.45… ``` ::: ::: [Save]{style="color:darkorange"} the `processed_data`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} write_xlsx(x = processed_data, path = "output/processed_data.xlsx") ``` ::: # Practice time! Save the resulting dataset as `yrbss_filtered`. 2. Use `rename()` to rename 2 of the column names of `yrbss_filtered` to something more meaningful. 3. Use `select()` to keep only the columns that denote record, sex, grade, bmi, and the 2 renamed columns. 4. Arrange the data by bmi, starting with the highest value and ending with the least value. 5. Save the resulting data set in the file `data/practice4_data.xslx`. # Make new variables: `mutate()` ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_mutate.jpg){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![[data transformation cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/master/data-transformation.pdf){target="_blank"}](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_makenewvars.png){fig-align="center"} ::: ::: ## `mutate()` - A confusing name, a powerful `{dplyr}` verb ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - You can use `mutate()` to - transform existing columns (variables in your dataset) and/or - add new columns to the dataset. ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_mutate.jpg){fig-align="center" width="70%"} ::: ::: Think of this like adding a formula in Excel to calculate the value of a new column based on previous columns. You can do lots of things such as: - subtract one column from another - convert the units of one column to new units (such as days to years) - change the capitalization of categories in a variable - recode a continuous variable to be a categorical one ## Calculate a new variable based on other variables with `mutate` ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Example 1 - Convert the weights to kg, assuming `stweight` is in lbs ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_data |> mutate(weight_kg = stweight/2.205) |> glimpse() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 20,000 Columns: 9 $ record 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 131706… $ age "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years … $ sex "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", … $ grade "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11t… $ race4 "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi… $ race7 "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi… $ bmi 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.844… $ stweight 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.4… $ weight_kg 31.26984, 22.63039, 41.14286, 25.51020, 30.85714, 23.65533, … ``` ::: ::: :::{.callout-note} - Notice we use `=` inside mutate, not `==` or `<-`. - We are not making a logical operation, nor are we assigning something to an object name. ::: :::{.callout-important} Since we haven't assigned this new "mutated" dataset to an object name, our work hasn't been saved. ::: ### Example 2 1. Convert the weights to kg, assuming `stweight` is in lbs (same as in Ex 1) 2. Convert the weights to g, using the kg weights 3. Create a new dataset (`yrbss_new`) with the new variables ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_data |> mutate(weight_kg = stweight/2.205, weight_g = weight_kg*1000) glimpse(yrbss_new) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 20,000 Columns: 10 $ record 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 131706… $ age "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years … $ sex "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", … $ grade "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11t… $ race4 "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi… $ race7 "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi… $ bmi 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.844… $ stweight 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.4… $ weight_kg 31.26984, 22.63039, 41.14286, 25.51020, 30.85714, 23.65533, … $ weight_g 31269.84, 22630.39, 41142.86, 25510.20, 30857.14, 23655.33, … ``` ::: ::: - We saved the new dataset as `yrbss_new` - to avoid overwriting our other data set. - Check out the environment tab to see the new dataset listed. - Notice how we can add multiple new columns within one `mutate()` function. - We can also mutate using multiple columns. - Here we have created two new weight variables in different scales - In the next *Practice time*, you will make a height variable from `bmi` and `weight_kg` ### Examine new columns Figure comparing the newly created `weight_kg` column to the original `stweight` column: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = stweight, y = weight_kg) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-96-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ## Categorical data (`factor`s) ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Intro - One data type that we haven't yet looked at are `factor` variables - [factors are how R represents **levels** of *categorical data*]{style="color:darkorange"}. - For the most part, you can use `character` and `factors` interchangeably for categorical data. - However, there are two main differences: - `factor`s define the *permissible* values in a vector with an argument called `levels`. - The levels define the order in which the permissible values are displayed, such as in a table or figure. - The default order of levels (values) is in **alphanumeric** order. ### Make `grade` factor - Below we use the `factor()` function within a `mutate()` to transform `grade` from a `character` variable into a `factor` variable. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$grade) # what's the data type of grade? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade n percent valid_percent 10th 4907 0.24535 0.2504338 11th 4891 0.24455 0.2496172 12th 4577 0.22885 0.2335919 9th 5219 0.26095 0.2663570 406 0.02030 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> #create a factor variable mutate(grade_fac1 = factor(grade)) # Checks: class(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what's the data type of grade_fac1? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "factor" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what are its levels? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "10th" "11th" "12th" "9th" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # values are the same in the two grade variables: yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac1) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac1 grade 10th 11th 12th 9th NA_ 10th 4907 0 0 0 0 11th 0 4891 0 0 0 12th 0 0 4577 0 0 9th 0 0 0 5219 0 0 0 0 0 406 ``` ::: ::: ### Change order of levels - Use the `levels` argument within the `factor()` function Example: Create `grade_fac2` variable as a factor version of grade with grades in "correct" order: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> #create a factor variable mutate(grade_fac2 = factor( grade, levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th") )) # Checks: class(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what's the data type of grade_fac1? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "factor" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what are its levels? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "9th" "10th" "11th" "12th" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac2 n percent valid_percent 9th 5219 0.26095 0.2663570 10th 4907 0.24535 0.2504338 11th 4891 0.24455 0.2496172 12th 4577 0.22885 0.2335919 406 0.02030 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # values are the same in the two grade factor variables: yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac1, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac2 grade_fac1 9th 10th 11th 12th NA_ 10th 0 4907 0 0 0 11th 0 0 4891 0 0 12th 0 0 0 4577 0 9th 5219 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 406 ``` ::: ::: ### Be careful! - Recall that the `levels` of a factor are the *permissible values* in a `factor`. ::: callout-warning *Spelling matters!* ::: - If we try to use a factor level that doesn't exist ("10" vs "10th"), - we won't have any data in that category, and - the non-specified levels ("10") get set to `NA` (missing). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate(grade_fac3 = factor( grade, levels = c("9th","10","11th","12th") # typo!! )) # check levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac3) # what are its levels? ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "9th" "10" "11th" "12th" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac3 n percent valid_percent 9th 5219 0.26095 0.3553483 10 0 0.00000 0.0000000 11th 4891 0.24455 0.3330156 12th 4577 0.22885 0.3116361 5313 0.26565 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # compare yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2, grade_fac3) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac3 grade_fac2 9th 10 11th 12th NA_ 9th 5219 0 0 0 0 10th 0 0 0 0 4907 11th 0 0 4891 0 0 12th 0 0 0 4577 0 0 0 0 0 406 ``` ::: ::: ### Replace `grade` with factor version - Below we are doing something called *reassignment*: - We’re taking the previous values of our variable (`grade`), - doing something to them (making them a factor), and - then reassigning the variable `grade` to its new set of values. - *I don't recommend replacing a variable with new values until you are 100% positive that your code is correct!!* - I can't compare the new `grade` values to the original ones anymore to check my work ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate(grade = factor( grade, levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th") )) # compare to grade_fac2 (which we determined to be correct) yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` grade_fac2 grade 9th 10th 11th 12th NA_ 9th 5219 0 0 0 0 10th 0 4907 0 0 0 11th 0 0 4891 0 0 12th 0 0 0 4577 0 0 0 0 0 406 ``` ::: ::: ### Remove extra variables - This step isn't necessary, but we don't need the columns `grade_fac1`, `grade_fac1-grade_fac3` anymore. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} names(yrbss_new) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" [6] "race7" "bmi" "stweight" "weight_kg" "weight_g" [11] "grade_fac1" "grade_fac2" "grade_fac3" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> select(-grade_fac1:-grade_fac3) names(yrbss_new) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "record" "age" "sex" "grade" "race4" "race7" [7] "bmi" "stweight" "weight_kg" "weight_g" ``` ::: ::: ::: # Practice time! (part 5) 1. Create a new variable `height` that calculates height based on `bmi` and `weight_kg`. Note the formula for BMI is weight/height\^2 (weight is in kilograms and height is in meters). Create a scatterplot of `bmi` vs `height`. 2. Create a new factor variable `sex_fac` that orders the values of `sex` as Male, Female. # Make variables with `case_when()` ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/dplyr_case_when.png){fig-align="center"} ## `case_when()` ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Intro - How can we dichotomize a continuous variable, such as BMI? - How can we combine categories of a variable, such as race? - How can we convert categories to numbers, such as sex? These are all examples where `case_when()` can be used. ### Code - The code follows a **basic pattern** for each category inside `case_when()`: - [*condition* \~ *category name*]{style="color:darkorange"} - The *left* side of the `~` is where we specify how to *define* the category. - The *right* side of the `~` is where we specify the *category name*. - Separate each "case" with a comma. ### Example 1 ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` sex n percent valid_percent Female 9592 0.47960 0.4852041 Male 10177 0.50885 0.5147959 231 0.01155 NA ``` ::: ::: - Convert `sex` to a new numeric variable called `female` whose values are - 1 when `sex` is Female, and - 0 when `sex` is Male [*left side* defines the condition *\~* *right side* names the category]{style="color:darkorange"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( female = case_when( # when sex is Female, make female equal to 1 sex == "Female" ~ 1, # do not forget this comma! # when sex is Male, make female equal to 0 sex == "Male" ~ 0 ) ) # checks yrbss_new |> tabyl(female) # look at the categories ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` female n percent valid_percent 0 10177 0.50885 0.5147959 1 9592 0.47960 0.4852041 NA 231 0.01155 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$female) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "numeric" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # compare female and sex variables yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex, female) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` female sex 0 1 NA_ Female 0 9592 0 Male 10177 0 0 0 0 231 ``` ::: ::: - How were the missing values (`NA`) handled? ### Example 2 - Dichotomize the numeric variable `bmi` into two categories with 30 as the cutoff. [*left side* defines the condition *\~* *right side* names the category]{style="color:darkorange"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( bmi30 = case_when( bmi < 30 ~ "<30", # don't forget a comma between cases bmi >= 30 ~ "30+" ) ) # check yrbss_new |> tabyl(bmi30) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` bmi30 n percent valid_percent 30+ 1351 0.06755 0.0997637 <30 12191 0.60955 0.9002363 6458 0.32290 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$bmi30) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # check values were categorized correctly: (look at mins & maxes) yrbss_new |> group_by(bmi30) |> get_summary_stats(bmi) |> gt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
bmi30 variable n min max median q1 q3 iqr mad mean sd se ci
30+ bmi 1351 30.008 53.947 33.157 31.413 36.442 5.030 3.199 34.614 4.448 0.121 0.237
<30 bmi 12191 13.161 29.997 21.867 19.974 24.381 4.407 3.312 22.263 3.198 0.029 0.057
``` ::: ::: ### Example 3 - Recode `bmi` to have 4 levels: - `<20` (not including exactly 20) - `20-30` (not including exactly 30) - `30+` - `unknown` if missing - Code comments - Notice that for the middle category we use an `&` statement similar to how we used logic in filter - New: used `.default` option to specify what to assign in all other unspecified cases. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( bmi3cat = case_when( bmi < 20 ~ "<20", (bmi >=20) & (bmi < 30) ~ "20-30", bmi >= 30 ~ "30+", .default = "unknown" # apply this in all other cases ), bmi3cat = factor(bmi3cat) # make it factor after creating categories ) # check yrbss_new |> tabyl(bmi3cat) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` bmi3cat n percent <20 3167 0.15835 20-30 9024 0.45120 30+ 1351 0.06755 unknown 6458 0.32290 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$bmi3cat) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "factor" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # check values were categorized correctly: (look at mins & maxes) yrbss_new |> group_by(bmi3cat) |> get_summary_stats(bmi) |> gt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
bmi3cat variable n min max median q1 q3 iqr mad mean sd se ci
<20 bmi 3167 13.161 19.997 18.794 17.882 19.464 1.581 1.128 18.516 1.175 0.021 0.041
20-30 bmi 9024 20.007 29.997 23.030 21.475 25.201 3.726 2.707 23.578 2.583 0.027 0.053
30+ bmi 1351 30.008 53.947 33.157 31.413 36.442 5.030 3.199 34.614 4.448 0.121 0.237
``` ::: ::: ::: # Practice time! (part 6) 1. Create a new variable called `grade_num` in `yrbss_new` that converts `grade` to numeric. 2. Create a new variable called `race_cat` that relabels `race4` to have shorter race category names: W (White), H/L (Hispanic/Latino), B/AA (Black or African American), Other (All other races). Make `race_cat` a factor variable with levels ordered by the size of the group in descending order. 3. Create a `tabyl` of the new variable `race_cat` and `race4` to check your work, including the ordering of the factors. # Replace missing values with `replace_na()` ## `replace_na()` - Sometimes we want to fill in missing values with a certain value. - We can use the `replace_na()` function inside of `mutate()` to specify this. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Example 1 - For example, suppose if the bmi variable is missing, - we want to replace it with the average bmi across the entire data set. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( bmi_filled = replace_na( bmi, mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE)) ) # check yrbss_new |> select(contains("bmi")) |> tail() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 4 bmi bmi30 bmi3cat bmi_filled 1 NA unknown 23.5 2 NA unknown 23.5 3 NA unknown 23.5 4 NA unknown 23.5 5 NA unknown 23.5 6 NA unknown 23.5 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # how does bmi_filled compare to bmi? yrbss_new |> select(bmi, bmi_filled) |> get_summary_stats() |> select(-iqr, -se, -ci) |> gt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
variable n min max median q1 q3 mad mean sd
bmi 13542 13.161 53.947 22.325 20.144 25.668 3.835 23.495 4.988
bmi_filled 20000 13.161 53.947 23.495 21.190 23.857 2.258 23.495 4.104
``` ::: ::: ### Example 2 - We can also fill in missing values for character variables - Here we replace missingness in the `sex` column with `unknown`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$sex) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "character" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` sex n percent valid_percent Female 9592 0.47960 0.4852041 Male 10177 0.50885 0.5147959 231 0.01155 NA ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( sexfilled = replace_na(sex, "unknown")) # check yrbss_new |> tabyl(sexfilled, sex) |> adorn_title() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` sex sexfilled Female Male NA_ Female 9592 0 0 Male 0 10177 0 unknown 0 0 231 ``` ::: ::: ### Example 3 - What happens when we use `replace_na()` to fill in missing values for factor variables? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} class(yrbss_new$grade) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "factor" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> mutate( gradefilled = replace_na(grade, "unknown")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `mutate()`: ℹ In argument: `gradefilled = replace_na(grade, "unknown")`. Caused by error in `vec_assign()`: ! Can't convert from `replace` to `data` > due to loss of generality. • Locations: 1 ``` ::: ::: ::: # More data visualizations with `{ggplot2}` ## Boxplots with `geom_boxplot()` - Boxplots can be used to compare the distribution of a quantitative variable among categories. - We need a categorical variable (here, our `x` axis) - and a continuous numeric variable, shown here on our `y` axis. ::: {.cell output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # creating a boxplot ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = grade, y = bmi) + geom_boxplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-110-1.png){width=960} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Note that the grades are in the correct order now! ``` ::: - The main differences from the scatterplots we created earlier are the `geom` type and the variables plotted. - We can change the color similarly to scatterplots. - However, we map to `fill` and not `color` if we want to fill in the box with color: ::: {.cell output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} # adding color ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = grade, y = bmi, fill = grade) + geom_boxplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-111-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## Faceting our boxplot with `facet_wrap()` ::: columns ::: {.column width="35%"} - One of the most powerful ways to change a visualization is by *faceting*. - We can make multiple plots stratifying by a categorical variable. - To do this, we have to add the `facet_wrap()` function to our plot. - We need to specify the variable to `facet_wrap` - `race4` by using the `vars()` function to specify it as a variable. ::: ::: {.column width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # facet_wrap: ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = bmi, y = grade, fill = grade) + geom_boxplot() + facet_wrap(vars(factor(race4))) #NEW! ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-112-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: - *Don't try to facet on a continuous `numeric` variable - it won't work.* - Don't forget to look at the help documentation (e.g., `?facet_wrap`) to learn more about additional ways to customize your plots! ## Faceting our boxplot with `facet_grid()` ::: columns ::: {.column width="35%"} - Another way to facet our plots is with `facet_grid()`, - which lets us select the rows and columns based on categorical variables: ::: ::: {.column width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = bmi, y = sex, fill = sex) + geom_boxplot() + facet_grid(rows = vars(grade), #NEW! cols = vars(race4)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_R_berd_2026_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-113-1.png){width=1152} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Mini-practice Facet the boxplot below by `age` and `grade`, don't forget the `vars()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # change eval: TRUE above ggplot(yrbss_new) + aes(x = bmi, y = sex, fill = sex) + geom_boxplot() + facet_grid(______) ``` ::: # Practice time! (part 7) 1. Create a barplot of `race4`, faceted by `sex`. # Useful keyboard shortcuts [Full list of keyboard shortcuts](https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/articles/200711853-Keyboard-Shortcuts){target="_blank"} | action | mac | windows/linux | |-------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------| | Run code in qmd (or script) | cmd + enter | ctrl + enter | | `<-` | option + - | alt + - | | interrupt currently running code | esc | esc | | keyboard shortcut help | option + shift + k | alt + shift + k | **Practice:** Try typing code below in your qmd (with shortcut) and evaluating it (with shortcut): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y <- 5 y ``` ::: # Conclusions ![Artwork by \@allison_horst](image/horst_r_first_then.png){fig-align="center"} ## You WILL get frustrated while learning R! From Garrett Grolemund's Prologue of his book *Hands-On Programming with R*[^1]: [^1]: Grolemund, Garrett. 2014. Hands-on Programming with R. O’Reilly. [https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/](https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/){target="_blank"} > As you learn to program, you are going to get frustrated. You are learning a new language, and it will take time to become fluent. But frustration is not just natural, it’s actually a positive sign that you should watch for. Frustration is your brain’s way of being lazy; it’s trying to get you to quit and go do something easy or fun. If you want to get physically fitter, you need to push your body even though it complains. If you want to get better at programming, you’ll need to push your brain. Recognize when you get frustrated and see it as a good thing: you’re now stretching yourself. Push yourself a little further every day, and you’ll soon be a confident programmer. ## Where are you in your R learning timeline? ![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_timeline_Rlearning.png){fig-align="center"} # Resources ::: {.panel-tabset} ### R Resources - [R for Data Science](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/){target="_blank"} book by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund - [R Programming for Health Data Science](https://niederhausen.github.io/BSTA_526_W26/){target="_blank"} (BSTA 526) course webpage ### Quarto Resources - [*Creating Professional Presentations and Websites using R/Quarto*](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/Quarto_BERD_2025/){target="_blank"} - OCTRI-BERD workshop from July 2025 - [Official Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/){target="_blank"} - [Markdown basics](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html){target="_blank"} - Text formatting, headings, links, images, lists, tables, equations, diagrams, page breaks, keyboard shortcuts, and more! - [Code blocks](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html#code-blocks){target="_blank"} - [Code chunk options:](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html#chunk-options){target="_blank"} - Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel's [Quarto tip a day:](https://mine-cetinkaya-rundel.github.io/quarto-tip-a-day/){target="_blank"} ::: # Thank you!! <script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
